input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: I notice both jobs ran on .NET 10.0.8 — the job IDs weren't actually switching runtimes. Let me fix the configuration to use proper runtime monikers:
data.reasoningText:
The benchmarks ran, but I notice a problem: both the "net9.0" and "net10.0" jobs show "Runtime = .NET 10.0.8" - which means BenchmarkDotNet didn't actually use the .NET 9 runtime for the net9.0 job. This is because the Job configuration I used (Job.ShortRun.WithId("net9.0")) just sets an ID name, not the actual target framework.

I need to fix this to properly use BenchmarkDotNet's multi-targeting. The correct approach is to use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute with `RuntimeMoniker` or configure jobs with the proper runtime. Let me fix the Program.cs to use proper runtime targeting.


Actually, I should leverage BenchmarkDotNet's built-in multi-targeting support by using `Job.Default` with the appropriate `RuntimeMoniker` instead, since the project already targets multiple frameworks.
